original_hypothesis.title: ERC721 Cross Supply Decrease Missing Owner Verification Allows Accounting Corruption
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: VaultLogic.erc721DecreaseCrossSupply checks tokenData.supplyMode == SUPPLY_MODE_CROSS but does NOT verify tokenData.owner == user parameter. If a caller (via module code) passes a different user address than the actual token owner, the userScaledCrossSupply of the wrong user gets decremented while the actual owner's count stays inflated. This could lead to the owner retaining phantom collateral counting.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The erc721TransferCrossSupply function does verify ownership per token, but erc721DecreaseCrossSupply (used in withdrawals) only checks supplyMode. If the higher-level code passes the wrong user parameter, accounting is silently corrupted. The withdrawal validation does check tokenData.owner == onBehalf, so exploitation depends on whether there's a code path that bypasses this.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No require(tokenData.owner == user) check; only checks supplyMode

preconditions: A module code path passes incorrect user to erc721DecreaseCrossSupply
The higher-level validation is missing or bypassable for the specific code path

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: erc721DecreaseCrossSupply lacks owner verification; relies entirely on caller correctness
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If any module passes wrong user, accounting becomes inconsistent
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(tokenData.owner == user) in the decrease function itself

analysis_questions: Do all callers of erc721DecreaseCrossSupply correctly pass the token owner?
Is there any code path (liquidation, flash loan return, etc.) where the user parameter could diverge?
What downstream effects would mismatched userScaledCrossSupply have?
validation_requirements: Audit all call sites of erc721DecreaseCrossSupply
Verify each passes the correct user address
Test with intentionally wrong user to observe corruption

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a liquidation or transfer code path incorrectly passes the wrong user to erc721DecreaseCrossSupply, the original owner retains phantom collateral count while their tokens are removed. This inflates their collateral value in health factor calculations, allowing them to maintain borrows against non-existent collateral.
prerequisites: A module code path that passes wrong user address

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Phantom collateral allows undercollateralized borrowing; bad debt for protocol
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires identifying and correcting mismatched accounting

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying owner parameter consistency across all erc721DecreaseCrossSupply callers

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction if exploitable code path exists
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing sum of user supplies vs total supply per asset

validation_result.mechanism: missing owner verification in ERC721 supply accounting
validation_result.impact_summary: erc721DecreaseCrossSupply lacks require(tokenData.owner == user), trusting callers to pass the correct user. If any module passes wrong user, userScaledCrossSupply accounting corrupts silently. Current visible callers appear to validate ownership, but the function itself is defective.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The function erc721DecreaseCrossSupply at VaultLogic.sol only verifies tokenData.supplyMode == SUPPLY_MODE_CROSS but does NOT verify tokenData.owner == user before decrementing userScaledCrossSupply[user]. This is inconsistent with sister functions: erc721TransferIsolateSupply checks require(tokenData.owner == from), and erc721DecreaseIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate reads tokenData.owner directly from storage rather than trusting a parameter. The missing check means if any caller passes an incorrect user address, the wrong user's supply count is decremented while the actual owner's count remains unchanged, violating the conservation invariant INV-ECON-002 (totalScaledCrossSupply == sum of all userScaledCrossSupply). The visible callers (validateWithdrawERC721 checks tokenData.owner == onBehalf, validateCrossLiquidateERC721 checks tokenData.owner == borrower) do validate ownership, but not all module source code is available (CrossLending, CrossLiquidation, BVault, IsolateLiquidation are referenced but source not fully provided). The function itself is missing a safety check that a consistent codebase would include.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct evidence at VaultLogic.sol erc721DecreaseCrossSupply shows no owner check. Comparison with erc721TransferIsolateSupply (has owner check) and erc721DecreaseIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate (reads owner from storage) confirms inconsistency. The same issue exists in erc721DecreaseIsolateSupply and erc721TransferCrossSupply which also lack owner verification.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No require(tokenData.owner == user) check. If user parameter doesn't match the actual token owner, userScaledCrossSupply[user] is decremented for the wrong user, while the actual owner retains phantom supply count.
This sister function DOES verify tokenData.owner == from, demonstrating that the pattern of owner verification exists in the codebase but was not applied to erc721DecreaseCrossSupply.

This safer pattern reads tokenData.owner directly from storage rather than trusting a caller parameter, ensuring the correct user's supply is decremented. erc721DecreaseCrossSupply should either follow this pattern or add an explicit owner check.

calling_sequence: User calls withdrawal via Proxy -> PoolManager.dispatch() -> module
Module calls ValidateLogic.validateWithdrawERC721 which checks tokenData.owner == onBehalf
erc721DecreaseCrossSupply only checks supplyMode, trusts user parameter
userScaledCrossSupply[user] decremented without verifying user matches tokenData.owner
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The function at VaultLogic.sol:335-349 accepts a user address parameter and decrements assetData.userScaledCrossSupply[user] without verifying tokenData.owner == user. While validateWithdrawERC721 at ValidateLogic.sol validates ownership, the library function itself is defective. For cross liquidation paths (ERC721), validateCrossLiquidateERC721 also checks ownership. However, not all module code is provided in scope - CrossLending, CrossLiquidation, IsolateLiquidation modules are referenced by ID but their full source executing withdrawal/liquidation logic is not available for exhaustive verification.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided relevant to this code defect

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario requires a code path that passes a wrong user to erc721DecreaseCrossSupply. From visible code, validateWithdrawERC721 and validateCrossLiquidateERC721 check ownership correctly. However, the function itself is defective - it should verify its own invariant (tokenData.owner == user) rather than relying entirely on caller correctness. The sister function erc721TransferIsolateSupply proves this check pattern exists in the codebase. Without complete module source (BVault, CrossLending, CrossLiquidation execution logic), we cannot rule out an unchecked path. The defect is confirmed as a missing safeguard; exploitability depends on all callers maintaining correctness, which is fragile.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If exploited (wrong user passed by any caller), the actual token owner retains phantom collateral in their userScaledCrossSupply count. This inflates their collateral value in health factor calculations (GenericLogic._getUserERC721BalanceInBaseCurrency uses assetData.userScaledCrossSupply), potentially allowing undercollateralized borrowing. The conservation invariant INV-ECON-002 (totalScaledCrossSupply == sum of userScaledCrossSupply) would also be violated since the wrong user's counter could underflow or go negative (revert in Solidity 0.8+). Severity is HIGH if any caller misuses the function.
recommended_mitigations: Add require(tokenData.owner == user, Errors.INVALID_TOKEN_OWNER) inside erc721DecreaseCrossSupply for each token in the loop, before clearing the owner
Apply the same fix to erc721DecreaseIsolateSupply which has the same missing check
Apply owner verification to erc721TransferCrossSupply (require tokenData.owner == from)
Consider refactoring erc721DecreaseCrossSupply to follow the pattern of erc721DecreaseIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate, reading the owner from storage rather than accepting it as a parameter
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy the full protocol with test NFTs and ERC20 assets. 2. Create a custom module or modify an existing one to call erc721DecreaseCrossSupply with a user address different from the token owner. 3. Observe that userScaledCrossSupply[wrong_user] decrements while actual owner's count stays inflated. 4. Verify the actual owner can still use their phantom supply as collateral for borrowing. 5. Check that the totalScaledCrossSupply no longer equals the sum of all user supplies.
uncertainties: Complete source code for CrossLending, CrossLiquidation, IsolateLiquidation, BVault modules is not available - these modules may contain additional call paths to erc721DecreaseCrossSupply
Future module upgrades could introduce call paths that bypass ownership validation at the module level
The SetSupplyMode operation (referenced in InputTypes but execution not in scope) may call decrease/increase functions
additional_testing_needed: Audit ALL callers of erc721DecreaseCrossSupply across all modules (BVault, CrossLending, CrossLiquidation, IsolateLiquidation)
Verify erc721DecreaseIsolateSupply callers similarly validate ownership
Fuzz test the user parameter across all ERC721 decrease/transfer functions to detect silent accounting corruption
Test SetSupplyMode code path which transitions between cross and isolate modes
